coredata.dc:title: Chapter 7 Carbon dots as a new class of light emitters for biomedical diagnostics and therapeutic applications

Ng, Yann H.
Ng, Sing M.
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) were discovered about a decade ago and categorized as a new class of nanomaterials with interesting photoluminescence properties. CDs exhibit strong fluorescence emission, high quantum yield, superior chemical stability, photostability, and biocompatibility. Owing to these unique properties, CDs are ideal alternatives to the organic dyes and semiconductor-based quantum dots as light emitters for biomedical diagnostics and therapeutic applications. This chapter aims to review the development of CDs achieved in these two major areas, with particular focus on biosensors and bioimaging as tools for diagnostics, and drug deliveries and therapies for the therapeutic applications. Fundamental information regarding CDs that includes their optical properties, synthesis routes and bioengineering strategies are included to ease the understanding on the topics. Special highlights based on literature reports are also included, which cover on the different approaches for surface functionalization, molecular imprinting polymeric techniques, doping strategies, and the latest progress on the utilization of CDs for biomedical diagnostics and therapeutic applications.

chapter.ce:label: Chapter 7
chapter.ce:title: Carbon dots as a new class of light emitters for biomedical diagnostics and therapeutic applications

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Swinburne University of Technology Sarawak Campus, Kuching, Sarawak, Malaysia

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Swinburne University of Technology Sarawak Campus

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) were discovered about a decade ago and categorized as a new class of nanomaterials with interesting photoluminescence properties. CDs exhibit strong fluorescence emission, high quantum yield, superior chemical stability, photostability, and biocompatibility. Owing to these unique properties, CDs are ideal alternatives to the organic dyes and semiconductor-based quantum dots as light emitters for biomedical diagnostics and therapeutic applications. This chapter aims to review the development of CDs achieved in these two major areas, with particular focus on biosensors and bioimaging as tools for diagnostics, and drug deliveries and therapies for the therapeutic applications. Fundamental information regarding CDs that includes their optical properties, synthesis routes and bioengineering strategies are included to ease the understanding on the topics. Special highlights based on literature reports are also included, which cover on the different approaches for surface functionalization, molecular imprinting polymeric techniques, doping strategies, and the latest progress on the utilization of CDs for biomedical diagnostics and therapeutic applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Quantum dots (QDs) are well studied for optical applications, due to their strong and specific excitation-dependent fluorescence emissions. Although it has been more than a decade since its discovery, the cytotoxicity risks brought about by QDs, especially those containing heavy metals such as cadmium, has yet to be resolved. Therefore, less toxic and more biologically compatible alternatives of light emitters are needed for sensitive applications, particularly in the biomedical field that deals with human subjects. The recent discovery of carbon dots (CDs), which are made of majority carbon, has been a good alternative due to their excellent biocompatibility while portraying similar optical properties to the QDs.

CDs were discovered in a serendipitous event of electrophoretic purification of single-walled carbon nanotubes (SWCNTs) synthesized from arc-discharge soot (Xu et al., 2004). Initially, the main intention was to obtain SWCNTs via the oxidation of arc-discharged soot using strong nitric acid (HNO3) for a prolonged period. The acid oxidation step was aimed to remove the amorphous carbon and to improve the hydrophilicity by introducing functional groups to the SWCNTs. After performing gel electrophoresis, three obvious bands were observed: a fast moving and highly fluorescent band; a slow-moving dark band which was then determined to be short, irregular and tubular material; and purified SWCNTs that did not penetrate the gel matrix. The fast moving fluorescent band was unusual for SWCNTs and therefore initiated further investigation, which led to the discovery of CDs. The origin of the fluorescence was suggested from the quantum confinement effect brought about by its small size. In 2006, these carbon nanoparticles have been categorized as a new group of fluorescent nanoparticles and termed as “carbon dots” (Sun et al., 2006).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Unique Optical Properties and Other Basic Characteristics
ce:section.ce:para: Since the discovery of CDs, various studies have been carried out to characterize and investigate their optical and physiochemical properties. They have then quickly emerged as a good alternative to the existing QDs for various applications. This is mainly due to the lower toxicity risks to the environment and living things. Some of the unique optical properties and basic characteristics are described in this section.

The optical properties of nanoparticles are often sensitive to their morphology profile, concentration, agglomeration state and refractive index near the surface of the particle. Therefore, the UV-Vis spectroscopy becomes a useful tool to study nanomaterials, especially the insights of the structural properties. In the case of CDs, the core makeup of carbon often causes a strong absorbance in the UV region, with a tail extending into the visible range (Fig. 7.1A ). This has been observed in most of the CDs synthesized from laser ablation that were further surface passivated or treated with acid (Sun et al., 2006; Cao et al., 2007). CDs synthesized via a supported route that employed surfactant-modified silica nanospheres as carriers (Liu et al., 2009) and that produced from pyrolysis of sago waste (Tan et al., 2014) have also shown to have similar absorption profiles. However, there are some studies that have reported a slightly different UV-Vis absorption profile with a strong and well defined peak around the UVC region (200–280nm), as shown in Fig. 7.1B (Mohd Yazid et al., 2013; Thambiraj and Shankaran, 2016). Another group has recorded an absorption spectrum with an edge at around 325nm and a strong narrow peak at 245nm for the CDs produced from microwave heating of poly(ethylene glycol) (PEG) (Jaiswal et al., 2012). They have suggested the peak to be ascribed to the π–π* transition.

The absorbance profile is often affected by the presence of a dopant. For instance, the absorbance of N-doped CDs can be enhanced in the near-UV region, as shown in Fig. 7.1C. The N-doped CDs have showed stronger absorption at around 350nm as compared to Mg doped CDs. This strong absorption peak can be assigned as due to the n-π* transition, which suggested the presence of an aromatic π system with extended conjugation in the CDs structures. The n and π* can be originated from the amide (–CONH–) bond in aromatic structures (Liu et al., 2016b).

Fluorescence is one of the most attractive and unique optical properties of CDs. In general, most CDs emit blue fluorescence, with a peak in the 400 to 500nm region upon excitation by UV light (Sahu et al., 2012; Long et al., 2012; De and Karak, 2013; Hu et al., 2014b; Huang et al., 2014; Mehta et al., 2014; Mehta et al., 2015). Uniquely, some CDs showed excitation-dependent fluorescence emission with a red shift in emission when excited at longer wavelengths, as shown in Fig. 7.2A (Sun et al., 2006; Cao et al., 2007; Bourlinos et al., 2008a,b). However, those CDs that were codoped with Mg and N have exhibited nonshifting fluorescence emission upon excitation with different wavelengths in the UVA region (Fig. 7.2B). Similar results were also observed for CDs that have been codoped with boron (B) and sulfur (S) (Li et al., 2015; Ye et al., 2016).

Although most of the studies have reported blue-emitting CDs, it is still desirable to produce CDs that can emit longer wavelengths. This can reduce the background noise from the UV excitation wavelength, while also enabling excitation in the visible range using far cheaper light sources, compared to the UV light sources. CDs with red-shifted emission were synthesized via redox pathway using phosphoric acid (H3PO4) and sodium borohydride (NaBH4) (Hu et al., 2015). The starting precursors were either citric acid (CA) or ethylene glycol (EG) that were mixed with ethylenediamine end-capped polyethylenimine (PEI-EC) as a passivating agent. They found that EG mixed with PEI-EC and then oxidized by H3PO4 route yielded better red-shifting effect, while adding NaBH4 to CA solutions allowed more efficient tuning of the emission from violet to yellow. This could be due to the different degree of dehydrogenations during the carbonization. Another possible approach to red-shift the emission is by changing the compositions of the carbon paste electrodes during the synthesis of CDs via electrochemical method. CDs synthesized from a lower percentage of carbon paste electrode (64%) showed a higher degree of oxidation and the maximum emission wavelength has shifted with the excitation ranging from 280 to 380nm (Long et al., 2012). Thereafter, it is suggested that a higher degree of oxidation can lead to more complex surface states that could trap the excitons under excitations (Bao et al., 2015).

Although the origin of fluorescence for CDs has yet to be fully understood, a few acceptable explanations have been suggested. Quantum confinement due to the small size is one of the first ideas that has been proposed, based on its analogy to the well-studied QDs. Dong and coworkers have shown that the fluorescence of the oxidized CDs were size-dependent, as both excitation and emission spectra have shown a red shift when the molecular weight of the CDs has increased (Dong et al., 2010). It was also reported in their study that the degree of oxidation was not affecting the fluorescence of CDs. However, other studies have proven that reduction and oxidation treatments can alter the fluorescence emission of CDs. For instance, the reduction using NaBH4 yielded strong blue emission (~450nm) while oxidation with HNO3 treatment caused the emission in green region (~520nm) (Zheng et al., 2011). The formation of surface traps as a result of higher oxidation levels during surface passivation has led to a longer wavelength emission (Bae et al., 2004; Xu et al., 2013a). No conclusive explanation can be drawn from all these studies as the works were done separately under different experimental conditions.

Another possible suggestion for the fluorescence origin is the presence of surface energy traps on surface defects resulting from the passivation step. A study has been carried out to investigate this effect by varying surface defects via oxidation process and to correlate to the subsequent fluorescence profile from these CDs (Wei and Qiu, 2015). The CDs chosen were of similar sizes to ensure the quantum confinement effect remained constant. The results demonstrated that surface defects formed by the presence of oxygen-rich terminals have led to the emission at 520nm, while another emission at 450nm was due to the quantum size effect. Such findings have given an additional insight on the origin of fluorescence of CDs.

Quantum yield is defined as the efficiency of converting absorbed light into emitted light, which can be in the form of fluorescence. Fluorophores with high QY often emit strong fluorescence, even at low concentration. This reduces the amount of fluorophores required for an application and this subsequently can cut financial cost, besides promoting better clearance from body for in vivo applications. There is a wide range of QY values for CDs; the commonly reported ones are below 10%, especially those bare CDs that are obtained without surface passivation. Some studies have found that the QY value of CDs can be altered via surface functionalization or doping approaches (Wang et al., 2010; Liu et al., 2016c). Most of these studies have observed enhancement in fluorescence intensity and QY value. For instance, QY of N-doped CDs was improved by varying the content of carboxyl groups (Wang et al., 2015a), whereas the doping of N into CDs produced from hydrothermal treatment of m-aminobenzoic acid have yielded high QY of 30.7% (Wang et al., 2017).

Purification of a crude CD sample can isolate batches of CDs with different QYs. Often, CDs of similar physiochemical properties will have similar QY value and can be separated during purification. This has been demonstrated by Wang and coworkers where the purification of CDs functionalized with oligomeric PEG diamine (PEG1500N) was found to show no significant change in the fluorescence profile, but each fraction has different QYs (Wang et al., 2010). The QY values become progressively higher in the later fractions and reached the values of 55–60% in the last fraction. Other similar work showed that purification using aqueous gel column (Sephadex G-100) has successfully harvested CDs with QYs up to 78%, which was more than double the QY before fractionation (Anilkumar et al., 2011).

Elemental analysis from X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) carried out in most of the reported studies has suggested that CDs are mainly made up of a few basic elements. The highest content is of course carbon which gives its name, followed by oxygen and small percentages of hydrogen and nitrogen. Bare CDs without surface passivation are often found to have a higher ratio of carbon content. Similarly, CDs obtained from hydrothermal treatment of natural resources such as foods and fruit peels also consist of higher percentage of carbon content (Lu et al., 2012; Sahu et al., 2012; De and Karak, 2013; Qin et al., 2013; Huang et al., 2014; Wang et al., 2015h, Wang et al., 2016b). In some cases, purified CDs that were isolated after refluxing with HNO3 can have higher oxygen content. For instance, the oxygen content in CDs isolated from candle soot has increased up to around 45% after being refluxed with HNO3 (Liu et al., 2007). Similarly, CDs obtained from 3-(3,4-dihydroxyphenyl)-L-alanine (L-DOPA) that were refluxed with HNO3 have shown a high content of oxygen (Xu et al., 2013a). This suggested that the ratio of oxygen to carbon can be significantly enhanced via oxidation using strong acid.

Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) technique is commonly used to analyze the functional groups available on CDs. It is very common to find carboxylic/carbonyl functionalities from CD samples, as evident from a peak in the range of 1600–1770cm−1, originating from the vibrational stretching of C=O (Liu et al., 2007; Peng and Travas-Sejdic, 2009; Qu et al., 2012). Taking CDs isolated from rose-heart radish as an example (Fig. 7.3 ), the peaks observed at 1648 and 1409cm−1 were attributed to asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibration of COO−, respectively (Liu et al., 2017). The broad peak at 3420cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibrations of –OH and –NH and the sharp peak at 1133cm−1 was suggested to be due to the bending vibrations of –CO. The small band at 2930cm−1 was assigned to the stretching vibrational mode of C-H bonds. These are generally the characteristic peaks for CDs, although slight variation is possible, depending on the chosen starting materials and synthesis method. Generally, it can be concluded that the structure of CDs have a nanocrystalline core of graphitic sp2 carbon atoms and sp3 carbon defects (Liu et al., 2007; Yang et al., 2011; Wang et al., 2012a), which are functionalized with peripheral carboxylic/carbonyl moieties.

CDs are discrete quasispherical nanoparticles, where the size and morphology can be typically characterized using scanning electron microscopy (SEM), transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and atomic-force microscopy (AFM) (Baker and Baker, 2010). Most of the CDs reported have narrow size distribution, with diameters around or below 10nm (Mao et al., 2010; Bao et al., 2011; Wang et al., 2012a; Wu et al., 2012; Xu et al., 2013b; Hu et al., 2014a; Pan et al., 2015). In terms of morphology, CDs are often observed to be nearly spherical under TEM, as shown in Fig. 7.4 . The size distributions can be above 10nm as well, and generally still loosely accepted in the category of CDs (Chin et al., 2012; Fong et al., 2015).

Most of the CDs were found to be amorphous, regardless of the synthesis method (Liu et al., 2009; Tong et al., 2015). This can be confirmed from TEM images and selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern. The absence of discernible lattice structures on TEM images is a clear indication for amorphous characteristic. This can be further confirmed by the diffuse ring pattern observed from SAED. Nonetheless, there are also some CDs that are shown to portray the characteristic of weak crystallinity and graphitic nature (Sahu et al., 2012; Basu et al., 2015). This characteristic can be deduced from the measurement of lattice spacing that, typically having the value at about 0.25nm, corresponds to the (100) planes of graphite as shown in Fig. 7.4 (Zheng et al., 2015c). It was further confirmed by XRD data that depicted a very broad peak at 2θ of 23° and the SAED pattern that showed a broad diffraction ring structure, indicative of weak crystalline characteristics (Fig. 7.4).

Despite the unique optical properties of CDs, they are deemed to be ineffective if shown to have high toxicity towards living cells. This is especially a great concern for biomedical applications. Commonly, toxicity can be tested using a cell viability assay that employs the 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) assay. An MTT assay is a colorimetric assay that detects the color change from yellow of the tetrazolium dye to purple due to the formation of formazan in the presence of viable cells with active metabolism. The presence of formazan can be monitored at the absorbance maximum of 570nm. One of the early studies has evaluated the cytotoxicity effect of CDs on the proliferation, viability, and mortality of human breast cancer MCF-7 and human colorectal adenocarcinoma HT-29 cells (Yang et al., 2009c). The study found no significant cytotoxicity effect on both the cell lines. Toxicity testing can be performed in vivo as well using animal models. For instance, mice exposed to 8 and 40mg carbon core-equivalent/kg body weight survived during a 4-week experiment without any irregular symptoms or behavior (Yang et al., 2009c). Further histopathological assays tested for the kidney functions and potential heptic injuries have showed no significant effect as well towards the control group.

Typically, CDs reported in the literature have been proven to have little or no significant reduction in cell viabilities upon treatment with CDs (Wang et al., 2011c; Huang et al., 2012a; Lin et al., 2014; Edison et al., 2016). Extended investigations were also performed on surface functionalized CDs. In one study, neutral charge PEG functionalized CDs showed no abnormalities to standard mouse fibroblasts (NIH/3T3) in the cell morphology, intracellular trafficking, and cell cycle up to concentrations of 300µg/mL (Havrdova et al., 2016). However, pristine modified CDs were found to arrest the G2/M phase of the cell cycle, stimulating proliferation which led to higher oxidative stress although CDs remained outside the cell nucleus. The CDs were most cytotoxic when functionalized with positively charged PEI, as they managed to penetrate into the cell nucleus and induced changes in G0/G1 and G2/M phases of cell cycle, even at low concentrations.

In vivo bio-distribution is another concern when proposing to use CDs for biomedical applications. Tao and coworkers have labeled CDs with radioactive isotope 125I and found that less than 20% of 125I has detached from CDs in the mouse plasma over the period of 7 days (Tao et al., 2012). Based on the gamma counter measurement of various organs and tissues of the mice being injected, the accumulation of CDs mainly occurred in the reticuloendothelial system (RES) organs, such as liver and spleen. Furthermore, the kidney uptake of CDs was found to be considerably high at early time points, due to the small size (2–5nm). This suggests that CDs are most likely to pass the glomerulus and be excreted by urine. This was supported by the bio-distribution data that showed the decrease in radioactivity in all organs that were likely due to the clearance of CDs from the mouse (Tao et al., 2012).

Tumor uptake of CDs has also been explored in a study using near-infrared (NIR) fluorescence (Huang et al., 2013b). A sample was prepared by the coupling of CDs with a NIR dye, ZW800, and further injected into athymic nude mice bearing subcutaneous squamous cell carcinoma, SCC7, tumors. The tumor was easily distinguishable from the surrounding healthy tissue at 2h post-injection and the tumor signal remained high over time. Most of the remaining CDs-ZW800 particles were excreted through renal filtration, indicated by the high tumor-to-background ratio and low fluorescence level in other tissues and organs. These findings have shown the great potential of CDs for targeted study and clinical translation.

Since the discovery of CDs, various synthetic routes to produce CDs in more timely and cost-effective manner have been investigated. Similar to QDs, CDs can be generally produced via two major approaches: top-down and bottom-up methods.

In this approach, CDs are produced by breaking down a larger carbon structure into nano-sized particles using powerful techniques such as lithography or etching (Baker and Baker, 2010). Few other studies were attempted using arc-discharge method, laser ablation, and electrochemical. Sun and coworkers have produced CDs from laser ablation of a carbon target in the presence of water vapor using argon as carrier gas (Sun et al., 2006). The crude sample showed no fluorescence, although consisted of majority nanoscale particles. Passivation with HNO3 solution was found crucial to introducing the fluorescence property. Further passivation using diamine-terminated oligomeric poly(ethylene glycol) PEG1500N by heating at 120°C for 72h was found to improve the QY from 4% to more than 10%. Adopting a similar concept, the synthesis and surface-modification of the CDs can occur simultaneously by selecting the right organic solvent (Hu et al., 2009). In the work, graphite powder with an average size of 2µm was first dispersed separately in three solvents: diamine hydrate, diethanolamine, and poly(ethylene glycol) PEG200N respectively and then irradiated with laser. PEG200N has contributed to the surface modification by yielding carboxyl-rich CDs that emitted in the visible range, as shown in Fig. 7.5 . CDs obtained from the other organic solvents showed higher QY with a shift in absorption and fluorescence spectrum profiles. It has also been proven that the CDs size can be controlled by tuning the laser pulse width (Hu et al., 2011). The long pulse-width laser in the range of millisecond was found a better option to regulate and control the laser-induced physical processes. CDs produced using shorter pulse width (0.3 ms) were shown to have higher QY and smaller sizes (averagely 3.2nm), compared to that with longer pulse width (1.5 ms).

Electrochemical (EC) etching technique has also been employed for the preparation of CDs (Bao et al., 2011; Ming et al., 2012; Deng et al., 2014). Typically, a carbon source will be electrochemically etched by electrodes that are controlled at constant potential using direct current power supply. In some cases, carbon fibers can act as a starting source-cum-working electrode, while a platinum (Pt) sheet or silver (Ag) wire can be the counter electrode and quasi-reference electrode, respectively (Bao et al., 2011). The EC method is able to tune the degree of surface oxidation and the size of CDs. Higher applied potential was found to reduce the size of the resulting CDs. Furthermore, the CDs with narrow size distribution could be directly yielded without further separation step. For this, EC method could be used for large scale synthesis using two graphite rods as the anode and counter-electrode (Ming et al., 2012). After 120h of continuous stirring of the solution, the dark-yellow solution collected were filtered and centrifuged to isolate water soluble CDs with the size range of 3–6nm.

CDs produced from top-down synthetic approaches often have no detectable fluorescence. Thus, further reflux with strong acid such as HNO3 and H2SO4 is required to introduce the fluorescence. Such reflux has been applied to large carbon structures such as those commercially available activated carbons (Dong et al., 2010; Qiao et al., 2010) and different types of carbon nanotubes (CNTs) such as SWNTs and MWNTs, and graphite (Tao et al., 2012). For instance, Tao and coworkers have oxidized the CNTs and graphite using a mixture of acids (H2SO4: HNO3=3:1) and refluxed for 24h (Tao et al., 2012). The collected CDs showed similar PL properties regardless of the starting material and possessed superior photostability.

The bottom-up assembly forms CDs from simple molecular carbon precursors by either thermal or chemical treatments. Despite longer hour of synthesis, this approach is often more cost-effective compared to the top-down approach which requires expensive instrument and costly carbon starting precursors. There is a wide variety of carbon-rich materials that can be used as starting precursors with some that are easily accessible. These range from candle soot (Liu et al., 2007), fruit peels (Zhou et al., 2012a; Prasannan and Imae, 2013) to commercially available chemicals such as L-ascorbic acid (Wu et al., 2012) and different carbohydrates (Peng and Travas-Sejdic, 2009; Wang et al., 2011b).

Peng and coworkers are one of the pioneer groups that have synthesized CDs using acid dehydration approach (Peng and Travas-Sejdic, 2009). They synthesized bare and passivated CDs from inexpensive carbohydrates in acidic solution. CDs were yielded via a simple dehydration step of the carbohydrate by concentrated H2SO4 and followed by further oxidation via refluxing in HNO3 for 12h. CDs produced were rich in carboxyl functional groups and could serve as suitable terminals for the grafting of other molecules for surface functionalization.

Hydrothermal synthesis is another commonly used method to synthesis CDs, which involves crystallization of substances in aqueous solution at elevated temperature and pressure. This is usually a single-step process where starting precursors will be placed in a tightly sealed reactor chamber and heated to high temperature for a prefixed period. For instance, CDs can be produced from hydrothermal treatment of glucose with monopotassium phosphate in an autoclave oven at 200°C for 12h (Yang et al., 2011). Similarly, N-doped CDs have been successfully prepared by heating the mixture of grass and water in a Teflon-lined autoclave at 180°C for 3h. The CDs were collected from the supernatant after centrifugation at 12000rpm for 10min to remove the large particles (Liu et al., 2012c). Usually, higher reaction temperature is in favor for CDs with smaller size and higher QY.

Besides hydrothermal approach using conventional oven or furnace for heating, microwave assisted hydrothermal also has been explored for the synthesis of CDs. The duration of synthesis can be significantly shortened since microwave heating is highly efficient. Typically, starting precursors will be prepared in a solvent and heated by microwave irradiation in an open vessel or hydrothermal setup. In most cases, passivation reagents will be added together with the starting precursors prior to the irradiation. Since microwave heating supplies high power (400 to 900W) and efficient heating, only a few minutes of irradiation is sufficient for the synthesis (Liu et al., 2011; Jaiswal et al., 2012; Jiang et al., 2012; Liu et al., 2012b; Mitra et al., 2012; Wang et al., 2012b; Zhai et al., 2012; Qin et al., 2013; Yang et al., 2015b). For instance, CDs can be produced by heating glycerol mixed with phosphate solution for 14min in a 750W domestic microwave (Wang et al., 2011b). The isolated sample showed an excitation-dependent emission which optimally emitted at 430nm. It has been observed that an increase in the time of microwave irradiation has shifted the UV-Vis absorption to shorter wavelengths and enhanced the intensity of emission.

Micro-plasma approach can be used as well to speed up the chemical reaction and to accelerate the formation of CDs (Huang et al., 2015b). It was demonstrated that the complete synthesis required shorter time while also using less amount of activator. Thermal activation using electron and radical injection helps to accelerate chemical reaction that typically requires high energy expenditures. The plasma-assisted route has successfully synthesized CDs of smaller size (~2.4nm) compared to that of chemical-only route (~3.5nm) (Huang et al., 2015b). In another example, CDs were synthesized in an ‘H’ type glass reactor using micro-plasma anode and Pt cathode electrode. The electrodes were supplied with current of 6mA and discharge voltage of about 2500V (Wang et al., 2015i). CA was selected as the molecular precursor in the presence of ethylenediamine as the addictive or passivating agent. The micro-plasma treatment was performed for different durations and the progress of the carbonization was clearly indicated by the change of color of the solution.

CDs can be produced also via supported approach that minimizes the aggregation or agglomeration phenomena and subsequently can promote a better control over the size of the CDs. A simple route to produce size-controlled CDs can be achieved using a solid host, NaY zeolite as the support system (Bourlinos et al., 2008a). The NaY zeolite was first ion-exchanged with 2,4-diaminophenol dihydrochloride before being thermally oxidized above the decomposition temperature of the zeolite-hosted organic species (300°C), leading to formation of C-ZEO. Etching with hydrofluoric acid will remove the zeolite matrix, releasing the quasispherical CDs with size of 4–6nm.

Adopting similar concept, a study has reported an aqueous route to synthesize multicolor CDs with size range of 1.5–2.5nm using advanced surfactant-modified silica spheres and resols (phenols/formalydehyde resins) as carbon precursors (Liu et al., 2009). The modified silica nanospheres acted as carriers in the synthesis and provided anchors for the polymerization of resols in the solution. This prevented aggregation from happening during pyrolysis at 900°C. Bare and oxidized CDs were not fluorescent but after passivation with PEG1500N, the CDs showed broad emission spectra ranging from 430 to 580nm. The emission was excitation dependent with average QY of 14.7%.

Doping is applied to introduce impurities into pure intrinsic semiconductor to modify the energy level (Hu and Zhang, 2006). This was also applied for CDs synthesis where hetero-atoms such as O, N, S and phosphorus (P) were doped into CDs. CDs were well reported to be doped with N via simple one-pot synthesis approach (Liu et al., 2012b,c; Ma et al., 2012; Zhang et al., 2012; Xu et al., 2013b; Gong et al., 2015; Tan et al., 2015). It was achieved by using N-containing compounds as starting precursors such as 3-(3-dihydroxyphenyl)-l-alamine (L-DOPA), L-histidine and L-arginine (Xu et al., 2013b). The N-carbon bonding due to doping process has improved the fluorescence emission.

Otherwise, doping can be achieved by mixing the desired dopants into the starting precursors during the carbonization stage. For instance, cobalt chloride as dopant can be mixed to a carbon precursor of 1-1(2-pyridylazo)-2-naphthol (PAN) to yield Codoped CDs via one-step solvothermal method (Zhang et al., 2017). Similarly, manganese (Mn) cations can be mixed to form chelation with PAN as knots in the carbonaceous backbone to form Mn-doped CDs (Wang et al., 2015g). The fluorescence emission was observed to be red shifted to 565nm compared to the usual reported fluorescence wavelength that were below 500nm. Since red shifted, it has greater potentials to be used for cell imaging, fluorescence inks, anticorrosion fluorescent thin films, and chemosensors due to the reduction of noise from the excitation UV.

Inorganic salts doping were also possible and often discovered to improve the QY of CDs. The doping with inorganic salts namely ZnO and ZnS prior to passivation by organic molecules was found to have enhanced QY (Sun et al., 2008). In that study, ZnO- or ZnS-doped CDs were pre-treated with 1% sodium dodecyl sulfate and functionalized with PEG1500N. Both the ZnO-CDs and ZnS-CDs emitted strong bluish green luminescence with high QY (~50%) upon excited.

It is promising to dope CDs with paramagnetic elements such as gadolinium (Gd) for bioimaging application. Gd-doped CDs to can be used for a dual fluorescent magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) probe (Bourlinos et al., 2012). The dopant, gadopentetic acid was mixed into tri(hydroxymethl)aminomethane (TRIS base) and betaine hydrochloride before pyrolysis process at 250°C in surrounding air. TRIS base was to form the carbon core while betaine as the surface ligand grafted via amide bonds to the core (Fig. 7.6 ). The carboxylic groups of gadopentetic acid will interact with TRIS base to provide molecular level mixing that facilitated a more homogenous immobilization of Gd into the CDs. The doped CDs showed strong T1-weighted MRI contrast that can be explored for multimodal imaging. A few other groups have also worked on the similar concept for MRI applications by exploiting the fluorescence of Gd-doped CDs (Liao et al., 2015; Chen et al., 2016a; Yuan et al., 2016b). Xu and coworkers have further modified the Gd-CDs with ariginine-glycine-aspartic acid (RGD) tripeptide to provide high binding affinity towards cancer cells for targeted imaging (Xu et al., 2014a).

CDs can be incorporated with other polymers or particles to form nanocomposites or nanohybrids to expand its practical usages for real applications. One of the pioneer works was synthesizing nanohybrids with core-shell architecture; CDs as shell while other nanoparticles of various chemical natures and morphologies as the core (Markova et al., 2012). The core substrates were reacted with CDs at basic conditions (pH 8 to 12) for 1h using mass ratios of 1:1 or 5:1 (CDs: substrate). pH can be controlled to suit the different stabilities of substrates and the dissolution properties. Such hybrids have showed collective functionalities with fluorescence from CDs and magnetic field from biogenic magnetite, silver nanoparticles and carbon nanotubes (CNTs). Similar approaches can be applied easily to derive a wide range of fluorescent CDs-based nanohybrids as summarized in Table 7.1 .

The synthesis of CDs often requires extraction or purification step to exclude impurities. There are few adopted methods and centrifugation is the commonly reported one. It works by high speed spinning of a crude sample after carbonization to separate the fine CDs from those bulk impurities. Crude sample is often dispersed in water or other suitable solvents before the spinning. The centrifugation process can be repeated few rounds for better separation efficiency (Zhang et al., 2013b, Guo et al., 2014). Supernatant collected will be dried to isolate the CDs.

Filtration is another quick and simple technique to separate the CDs from the agglomerated impurities. This can be performed via microfiltration using membrane filter with small pore size such the one with 0.22 μm pores (Wang et al., 2015e). Similar to filtration, separation can be done by dialysis, where the crude sample is placed in a dialysis membrane tube and soaked in water or a suitable solvent. The molecular weight cut-off of the membrane can be chosen to be low such as at 1kDa (Lai et al., 2012; Liang et al., 2015). This method is typically effective to remove small impurities such as salts, especially for CDs sample produced from acid carbonization that involves neutralization process. However, dialysis is a lengthy process and requires copious amount of dialysate to remove the impurities.

Specifically for the removal of high salts content after neutralization, Ng and coworkers have introduced an alternative efficient approach using the organic-aqueous phase separation concept (Fong et al., 2015; Chang et al., 2016). Acetone was added to the CDs solution containing salts, where the water-soluble salts will remained in the aqueous phase while the comparatively less water soluble CDs will be extracted into the acetone. Extraction can be performed several times to ensure majority of CDs was extracted. The acetone can be removed easily in vacuum and the dried CDs could then be re-suspended in water for further analysis.

Fluorophores used in biomedical fields are often modified to achieve better biocompatibility and to be more efficient in targeting specific sites in vitro or in vivo. Biological engineering strategies can be applied based on Green Chemistry principles such as using less toxic starting materials and reagents. As for CDs, some of the reported strategies are such as functionalizing the surface with biocompatible compounds, encapsulating CDs into another matrix and forming molecularly imprinted polymers together with CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Alternatives for Greener Materials and Methods
ce:section.ce:para: Some fluorophores contain biologically incompatible compounds and one good example is the QDs that typically consist of harmful heavy metals. Therefore, biological engineering strategies are required to impart biological functionalities to the light emitters. In the case of CDs, the choice of starting material is diverse and selection can be made from those greener options such as biomass, food wastes or naturally occurring carbohydrate sources. Usage of nontoxic starting materials from the beginning can minimize the toxicity risks during the synthesis and subsequently the end product. Furthermore, the use of biomass and food wastes could promote recycling and reusing. Food wastes such as fruit peels (Zhou et al., 2012a; Prasannan and Imae, 2013; Tiong et al., 2015), frying oil (Hu et al., 2014c), chicken eggshells (Ke et al., 2014) and other food leftovers (Park et al., 2014) have been used as starting precursors for the synthesis of CDs. Biomass such as rice husk (Ngu et al., 2016), sago tree bark (Tan et al., 2014), carbohydrates (Chin et al., 2012; Briscoe et al., 2015; Fong et al., 2015, 2016), and grass (Liu et al., 2012c) have also been excellent candidates for the production of CDs due to their carbon-rich nature. They are applicable for various synthetic routes, namely via hydrothermal, pyrolysis and acid hydrolysis. Interestingly, there was a report that used human urine as the starting precursor of CDs in a heat carbonization synthesis (Essner et al., 2016). The CDs were named as “pee-dots” by the group and showed potential for fluorescence sensing of heavy metals.

The synthesis of CDs can adopt greener methods as alternatives to replace typical approaches that involve harsh physical or chemical reactions. This includes direct extraction of CDs from commercially available food and beverage such as Nescafe® original instant coffee (Jiang et al., 2014), Tsingtao® beer (Wang et al., 2015h), milk (Han et al., 2014; Wang et al., 2014a, Wang and Zhou, 2014), and food caramels (Sk et al., 2012). The formation of CDs can occur during the manufacturing processes that are mostly subjected to high temperature and pressure for pasteurization or cooking purposes. This means CDs can be isolated during food processing without much additional effort, energy, or steps.

Surface functionalization of CDs with chemical and/or biological moieties can increase the water solubility and subsequently improve its biocompatibility. Most of the CDs synthesized from a bottom-up approach contain organic functionalities, such as hydroxyl, carbonyl, and carboxyl groups that mostly originated from the starting precursor and/or as the products of oxidation during the synthesis process. As for top-down approaches, biologically compatible ligands will be passivated onto the CDs. Some commonly used polymeric ligands are variations of polyols and polyamines, such as poly(propionylethylenimine-coethylenimine) (PPEI-EI) (Cao et al., 2007), PEG (Gonçalves and Esteves da Silva, 2010; Sachdev et al., 2013; Dong et al., 2015), and branched polyethylenimine (BPEI) (Dong et al., 2012; Zhao et al., 2014). Besides better biocompatibility, surface passivation in some cases has also found to improve the fluorescence property.

The presence of oxygen-rich moieties has allowed surface functionalization or bio-conjugation to take place more readily. The high electronegativity of oxygen induces polarization of electrons that can promote better chemical and physical interactions with external ligands. Covalent bonds can form via electrophilic addition or substitution mechanism, while physical attraction can occur via hydrogen bonding, physical van der Waals interaction or electrostatic forces. In addition, the commonly observed strong absorption of CDs in the UV range indicates the presence of double bonds and possibly electron-rich aromatic structure. This has been reported to promote the π–π stacking phenomenon, as observed between CDs and gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) (Liu et al., 2014). CDs can adsorb onto the surface of AuNPs forming self-assembled monolayers (SAM), which are later observed to enhance the fluorescence once the CDs are released, due to the interaction with thiol-containing biomolecules (Mandani et al., 2015). This has allowed monitoring of biothiols which is an important early diagnosis of a variety of diseases (Lin et al., 2010). Other than AuNPs, CDs can also coassemble with proteins such as enhanced green fluorescent protein (EGFP) via hydrophobic and electrostatic interactions (Zhang et al., 2016). This hybrid has been prepared for cellular delivery of EGFP in which such noncovalent binding allows the release of EGFP into the cytoplasm while protecting it against enzymatic hydrolysis.

In passivating CDs via covalent bond, the most common method is by cross-linking carboxylic group with primary amine to form “zero-length” amide bonds (Gao et al., 2004). This is usually mediated by carbodiimide compounds such as 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC), acting as the coupling agent. Careful control of pH is usually critical in the coupling protocol, especially involving pH-sensitive proteins. Although optional, N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) is often included in the coupling procedure to improve the efficiency and conjugation yield. This chemistry has been applied for conjugation of many biomolecules, such as aptamers (Su et al., 2014; Xu et al., 2012), antibodies (Ma et al., 2015), and glycoproteins (Li et al., 2010). Otherwise, CDs can be conjugated with peptides via resin such as benzotriazol-1-yl-oxytripyrrolidinophosphonium, hexafluorophosphate (PyBOP) and 1-hydroxybenzotriazole hydrate (HOBt) as the mediator. The peptide-conjugated CDs can be removed from the resin using trifluoroacetic acid (TFA) solution. This has been demonstrated for conjugation of trans-activator of transcription (TAT) peptide onto CDs for live cell nuclear imaging (Tan et al., 2015).

The aforementioned chemical strategy allows bio-conjugation of ligands via covalent link to CDs. The targeting ligands can be of small molecules, peptides, aptamers, and antibodies, which act as drug carriers, sensing, or imaging agents. For example, folic acid (FA) is widely used to detect cancer cells as it has high affinity towards folate receptor (FR) in overexpressed tumor cells (Chiu et al., 2016; Mewada et al., 2014; Lei et al., 2016). Folate receptor is known to be up-regulated in various cancer cells and typically of high amount at a later stage of cancer, which makes it a good detection biomarker (Song et al., 2012). Hyaluronic acid (HA) has been conjugated to CDs for a specific targeted delivery to liver that has been overexpressed with HA receptors (Goh et al., 2012). Another well-known target for cancer cells is transferrin (Tf) due to the overexpression of Tf receptors on cancer cell membranes (Qian et al., 2002; Li et al., 2010). A small chain peptide consisting of arginine, glycine and aspartic acid, known as the RGD peptide, has also been known to target cancer cells (Cai and Chen, 2008). Feng and coworkers have improvized on the common method of conjugating the targeting ligands to the surface of CDs. They have covered the targeting ligand, RGD, that has first been conjugated to CDs with monomethoxypolyethylene glycol (mPEG) to protect the ligand during blood circulation (Feng et al., 2016). The tumor extracellular microenvironment, such as the slightly acidic condition, can trigger the RGD peptide to surface and facilitate the ligand-receptor interaction between RGD-integrin αvβ3 for effective cancer cell targeting. Besides cancer cells, bacteria labeling can also be achieved by functionalizing targeting molecules, such as mannose, to CDs (Lai et al., 2016).

Encapsulation is commonly adopted in drug delivery systems to form a shell to protect a particular drug, and prevent it from leaching out before reaching the targeted site. The shell is usually made of hydrogel matrices or polymeric nanostructures that are able to entrap the targeted drug. Having a similar concept, the hybrid of encapsulation with CDs can be used for theranostics purpose. CDs can serve as the cross-linker for the carrier matrix while the fluorescence will allow tracking of the matrix as part of the diagnosis. Jia et al. have designed the counterpart, containing a targeting ligand and matrix before coupling CDs into the system via carbodiimide chemistry (Jia et al., 2016b). For therapeutic function, doxorubicin (DOX) as anticancer drug was loaded to the matrix via electrostatic interaction. Having this, real-time and noninvasive location tracking of the cancer cells can be performed while the drug can be delivered to the correct site. Similar work has reported on a noncovalent embedding of CDs within the chitosan-based hydrogel matrix that is later used for the encapsulation of an anticancer drug, 5-fluorouracil (5-FU) (Sachdev et al., 2016). This work also reported on CDs that have been cross-linked with ornithine methacrylamide (OrnAA) as the monomer during inverse microemulsion free radical polymerization process to prepare the nano-gel. It was then conjugated with FA as a targeting ligand and encapsulated dextran as the model drug (Li et al., 2016e). Other than encapsulation, CDs have been used as surface coating for TiO2 nanostructures before being loaded with the hydrophobic multi-therapeutic drug, curcumin (Sawant et al., 2016). The presence of CDs has shown to improve the biocompatibility of hydrophobic drug and enhanced the anticancer potential.

The MIP technique is a synthetic method to prepare polymers with specific molecular recognition properties and can be an excellent sensing receptor (Uzun and Turner, 2016). The fabrication involves the polymerization of monomers in the presence of target analyte, followed by its removal after the formation of the polymer, leaving a cavity of specific shape and size complementary to the analyte. This cavity serves as a good binding site to recognize the targeted analytes. This technology can be merged into the fabrication of CDs that have specific recognition sites. The CDs can act as signal transducers that convert binding activity into readable optical signal. This is the change in optical property before and after the binding of analytes on the MIPs. This has been demonstrated for the detection of dopamine using MIP film anchored onto the surface of CDs (Mao et al., 2012). The work adopted a one-pot sol-gel polymerization approach that mixed the dopamine template together with 3-aminopropyl-triethoxysilane (APTES) and tetraethoxysilane (TEOS), followed by cross-linking via base-catalyzed hydrolysis and condensation reactions. The probe has shown effective quenching when the dopamine was introduced, while no quenching was observed for the nonimprinted polymer (NIP). The NIP was prepared with similar steps but without dopamine. A similar polymerization strategy has been adopted for fluorescence screening of other target molecules in real samples, such as sterigmatocystin in grains (Xu et al., 2016a), tetracycline in milk (Hou et al., 2016), α-amanitin sensing in blood serum (Feng et al., 2015), and bisphenol A in river water (Liu et al., 2016a). A separate study has reported on the use of amino-CDs directly as the functional monomer and mesoporous silica as the imprinting matrix to simplify the imprinting process (Xu and Lu, 2016). When compared to the conventional imprinting method using APTES, the sensitivity for trinitrotoluene (TNT) detection has significantly enhanced.

The molecular imprinting technique has been further explored using an epitope imprinting method that is generally targeted for macromolecules (Rachkov and Minoura, 2000). In this approach, a small peptide fragment called an epitope was used as a template, instead of the whole protein. This method has been adopted to synthesize thermo-sensitive MIPs coated with CDs for selective binding of cytochrome C via specific interaction at the recognition cavities (Li et al., 2016b).

Biosensors are used to aid the diagnosis and prognosis of certain medical conditions or diseases, while also able to monitor the progress of disease infection or treatment via detection of biomarkers. They work similarly to bioassays, such as enzyme-linked immunosorbent assays (ELISA), but more efficient, reusable, robust, and can be used on-site. The development of biosensors has further evolved and witnessed the merging with other materials and technologies. CDs have also been investigated as light emitters for biosensors applications, which covers the development aspects in design approaches, sensing mechanisms, sensing probe optimization, and potential to diversify the detection of different analytes.

A biosensor is defined as “an analytical device incorporating a biological material (e.g. tissue, microorganisms, antibodies, natural products, cell receptors, enzymes, nucleic acids, etc.), a biologically derived material (e.g. recombinant antibodies, aptamers, engineered proteins, etc.), or a biomimic (e.g., synthetic receptors, combinatorial ligands, imprinted polymers, biomimetic catalysts, etc.), intimately associated with or integrated within a physicochemical transducer or transducing microsystem, which may be optical, electrochemical thermometric, piezoelectric, magnetic or micromechanical” (Turner et al., 1987). Optical transducing systems are commonly adopted in biosensors owing to their ease of use and accuracy (Ligler and Taitt, 2011). Recently, optical biosensors have been developed using CDs as the main fluorophore. This is mostly for immunosensors that make use of antibody-antigen interactions and aptasensors featuring the specificity between aptamer and analyte of interest. A simple CDs-based optical biosensor consists of a biological sensing receptor conjugated or tagged onto the CDs that work as the transducer, as illustrated in Fig. 7.7 . The CDs optical property will change in corresponding to the interaction process between the target analyte and the receptor. For example, the conjugation of the aptamer for Salmonella typhimurium on the surface of CDs has enabled fast detection of bacteria in egg samples (Wang et al., 2015d). The detection was based on the change in the fluorescence property when the aptamer CDs complex was incubated with Salmonella for an hour. Compared to the conventional bacterial cell plate count method, this optical method has shown consistent result with high specificity and accuracy.

Besides the simple design described, sandwich-type biosensor assembly with CDs has been suggested and demonstrated for thrombin sensing based on aptamers (Xu et al., 2012). The sensing strategy is, as illustrated in Fig. 7.8 , where two amino-modified thrombin aptamers, TBA15 and TBA29 were being adopted in the sensor design. TBA15 bound to the fibrinogen recognition site of thrombin was first modified on silica nanoparticles (SNPs) to form a thrombin-bound TBA15-SNP complex. Subsequently, TBA29 which coupled with the heparin-binding exo-site was conjugated to CDs forming TBA29-CDs. When the first complex was introduced to the TBA29-CDs, an aptamer sandwich SNPs-thrombin-CDs was formed. In the absence of thrombin, the formation of such sandwich assembly is not possible and therefore allowing the determination of thrombin via monitoring of fluorescence changes after the centrifugation and re-dispersion steps. The linear dynamic range of the sensor was reported from 0–100nM with detection of limit as low as 1nM.

Most of the CDs-based sensing systems adopt direct monitoring of fluorescence emission intensity, mainly fluorescence quenching that is also known as “turn-off” sensing. The quenching can be due to static or dynamic interactions between a target analyte with the CDs. The resulting signal change can be correlated with the concentration of the analyte and this enables the accurate quantification to be performed on the analyte. Huang and coworkers have used PEG-capped CDs to detect ceftazidime and cefepime, based on the quenching profile of the CDs (Huang et al., 2015c). The detection strategy was based on the hydrogen bonding interaction between the hydroxyl group on the surface of CDs and the amino group on the cefepime and ceftazidime. The degree of quenching recorded has a direct correlation with the concentration of ceftazidime and cefepime in dynamic linear range of 0.33–3.30 and 0.24–2.40µg/mL. Similarly, such fluorescence quenching–driven sensing was also adopted for detection of biomolecules (Huang et al., 2015a); explosives (Niu et al., 2013); metal ions (Hu et al., 2014a; Mohapatra et al., 2015; Xu et al., 2015b); and antibiotics (Mishra et al., 2016).

In a more advanced approach, the fluorescence “turn-off-on” option has been explored. The system is designed by first introducing a quencher to the CDs to turn off the fluorescence, which later the targeted analyte will bind to the quencher due to better binding affinity and subsequently restoring the fluorescence of the CDs. The interaction involved is usually based on the reversible noncovalent type and displacement of quencher away from the CDs, which can be induced by the target analyte. This sensing approach has been utilized for the detection of pyrophosphate ion (PPi), based on nano-assembly of CDs with the assistance of Ce(III) ions (Chai et al., 2015). As shown in Fig. 7.9 , the initially fluorescing CDs will form aggregates in the presence of Ce(III) and result in fluorescence quenching. Subsequently, the disaggregation of the CDs assembly can take place when added with PPi. The corresponding turn-on of the fluorescence signaling has allowed sensitive determination of PPi in the range of 0.3 to 29.7µM with detection limit of 0.1µM. The sensing system can be regenerated for reuse by simply removing the analytes via centrifugation to recover the free CDs. A similar concept has also been demonstrated for detection of histidine (Hou et al., 2015) and ascorbic acid (AA) (Fong et al., 2016).

Förster resonance energy transfer is a nonradiative transfer of energy from an excited donor fluorophore to a ground-state acceptor within close proximity (normally 1–10nm) via long-range dipole-dipole interactions (Sapsford et al., 2006; Clapp et al., 2006; Biskup et al., 2007). The acceptor will absorb the energy from the donor but not necessarily re-emit the energy as fluorescence. This scenario is often known as dark quenching (Sapsford et al., 2006). In spectral terms, Fthe RET process highly depends on the degree of spectral overlap between donor fluorescence emission and acceptor absorption. The process is also sensitive towards the donor-acceptor separation distance and their relative dipole orientations.

One of the pioneer works explored possibilities of using CDs as energy donor while graphene as the energy acceptor (Wei et al., 2012). The graphene (rGO) was noncovalently functionalized with 18-crown-6 ether (18C6E) while the CDs were covalently animated. The sensing system works according to a FRET model, as illustrated in Fig. 7.10 . The working mechanism is based on the tight binding of primary alkyl-ammonium with crown ether, which brings CDs and graphene into close proximity, followed by inducing of energy transfer. The initial fluorescence of CDs was quenched due to this energy transfer. The FRET process was then inhibited when the target potassium (K) was introduced to the system. The relationship between CDs fluorescence and concentration of KCl was reported as linear within the concentration range of 0.05–10.0mM, with a detection limit of 10µM.

Gold nanoparticles are often used in a FRET system as they are excellent candidates for energy acceptor, due to their broad absorption spectrum in the visible light. The band is often easily overlapping with the fluorescence emission of CDs. For instance, the fluorescence of amino-functionalized CDs was quenched by citrate-stabilized AuNPs as they form CDs-AuNPs molecular assembly due to the Au-N interaction (Dai et al., 2014). Specifically for this system, melamine that contains amino group can then recover the fluorescence upon covalent binding with AuNPs while disrupting the FRET effect between CDs and AuNPs. Improvising on this system, a dual-mode sensing for glutathione (GSH) has been demonstrated, with an additional monitoring of color change (Shi et al., 2014b). The presence of CDs not only transferred energy to AuNPs when they were in close proximity, but also induced the aggregation of AuNPs and CDs, which resulted in the color change. When GSH was introduced to the system, the fluorescence quenching was reduced, accompanied by color change due to the increase in distance of the interparticle between the AuNPs and CDs, and among the AuNPs, respectively. Both the color change and fluorescence restoration were linearly correlated with the concentration of GSH up to 3.0µM. The determination of GSH in human plasma sample has also been carried out using the proposed dual-mode sensor, with recovery rate within range of 81.0 to 104.3%. glutathione sensing has also been demonstrated by employing the FRET between CDs and MnO2 nanosheets (Cai et al., 2015). The fluorescence “turn-on” sensing of GSH was applied on diluted human serum with a recovery rate between 94.6 to 105.1% and detection limit as low as 300nM.

Photon-induced electron transfer is one of the two main mechanisms that leads to changes in fluorescence emission by distance-dependent fluorescence quenching between a fluorophore and a quenching moiety (Doose et al., 2009). The general format of a PET sensor consists of three components: fluorophore, spacer, and receptor, as shown in Fig. 7.11A . During relaxation of an excited fluorophore, electrons will transfer from the receptor to the fluorophore and resulted in no fluorescence (Fig. 7.11B). However, in the presence of an analyte that can interact with the receptor, the initial nonradiative electron transfer will be hindered and thus restore the fluorescence (Fig. 7.11C). It is important to note that the PET only occurs if the oxidation potential of the receptor is smaller in magnitude than that of the fluorophore (De silva et al., 2009).

CDs are excellent candidates for fluorescent PET sensors due to their electron donating ability in their photo-excited state (Wang et al., 2009). Most CDs-PET sensors are based on the “turn-off” mode where fluorescence was quenched due to electron transfer and the degree of quenching will be correlated to the analyte amount. For instance, the fluorescence of CDs can be quenched by hydroquinone, H2Q via PET between CDs and H2Q (Ni et al., 2015). This was facilitated by the easy oxidation of H2Q in alkaline solution in the presence of ambient oxygen. The oxidized H2Q acted as the electron acceptor for CDs. The strong noncovalent interactions, such as hydrogen bonding, bring H2Q close to the surface of CDs, allowing electron transfer from the photo-excited CDs to oxidized H2Q. The resulting fluorescence quenching enables detection of H2Q in the linear range of 0.1 to 50µM with detection limit of 0.1µM. The electron transfer process was validated by GSH which is a known reducing agent for H2Q into the quenched system. The presence of GSH has successfully reduced H2Q and subsequently stopped the PET process and recovered the fluorescence of CDs.

Chemiluminescence resonance energy transfer is a process of nonradiative or dipole-dipole energy transfer from a chemiluminescent (CL) donor to a suitable acceptor molecule (Huang and Ren, 2012). Chemiluminescence resonance energy transfer is very similar to FRET, except that no excitation light source is required since excitation occurs via oxidation. Lin and coworkers have reported on the CL property of CDs in the presence of peroxynitrous acid (ONOOH) (Lin et al., 2011). The CL mechanism of CDs induced by ONOOH is illustrated in Fig. 7.12 . The online mixing of nitrite and acidified hydrogen peroxide, H2O2 produced oxidant, ONOOH and OH• which served as the hole injector and converted CDs to R•+. Meanwhile, reaction between ONOOH and H2O2 in acidic condition produced O2-• which donated electrons to CDs. This caused the CDs to be negatively charged. The CL emission was then released as a result of electron-hole annihilation in or between the R•+ and R•−. It has been suggested that the energy transfer to CDs has taken place from the conversion of ONOOH to nitrate via the radical stage. This was possible due to the overlap of the light emission in the range of 350 to 450nm with the excitation wavelength of CDs. The enhancement of CL intensity has been utilized for nitrite sensing within a linear dynamic range of 0.1–10µM, with detection limit of 0.53nM. Since then, the same research group has further explored nitrite sensing using a slightly improved CRET system based on CDs (Lin et al., 2015). They have discovered that the CL from ONOOH-carbonate system can be significantly amplified. The CL energy transfer to CDs occurred from (CO2)2* which was generated as a result of decomposition of ONOOCO2−. It resulted in CL emission as the excited CDs return to the ground state. Similarly, CL intensity enhancement has also been exploited for detection of other analytes such as Fe(III) (Zhao et al., 2014), trimethylamine (TEA) (Zhang et al., 2015), and aliphatic primary amines (Zhou et al., 2012b).

Electrochemiluminescence is another option for electrons to transfer to a suitable acceptor without the need of an excitation light source. A chemical species that undergoes an high energy electron transfer reaction will get excited and subsequently produce luminescent emission (Miao, 2008). CDs have proven to exhibit superior ECL activities (Dong et al., 2010). The surface defects on CDs that were rich with oxygen-containing functional groups can serve as active sites to facilitate the ECL activities (Xu et al., 2013a). Surface passivation was found to reduce the ECL characteristics for CDs (Dong et al., 2010).

The immobilization of CDs on another platform, such as graphene, can amplify effectively the signal of ECL (Yang et al., 2013). In such a system, the ECL signal was reported to be enhanced by 48 times in the presence of a coreactant, S2O8 2−, compared to those without coreactant. Subsequently, the presence of chlorinated phenols was found to quench the ECL emission and can be used as sensor with an effective linear range between 1.0 and 1000pM. The detection limit was 1.0pM for pentachlorophenol. Based on a similar working principle, the ferrocyanide-ferricyanide redox couple (Fe(CN)6 3−/4−) was found to effectively induce the ECL of CDs (Niu et al., 2015). The CDs were polymerized with a cationic surfactant in which the polypyrrole of the surfactant has accelerated the electron transfer process. The redox couple worked by converting the CDs and coreactant S2O8 2− to negatively charged species of CDs•− and SO4•−. The ECL electron transfer has yielded 10-fold amplification in the ECL signal and enabled the development of a sensitive detection of GSH with limit of detection of 54.3nM, with dynamic response range of 0.1 to 1.0µM. Furthermore, the same system has been employed for the determination of iron ions, using the dual-peak ECL observed at -2.84V and -1.71V in the presence of 0.1M tetrabutyl ammonium bromide (TBAB) ethanol solution (Zhang et al., 2014). It was found to be more efficient in distinguishing the metal ions compared to single-peak systems. The first peak was a result of electron transfer reaction between reduced CDs and coreactants, where the electron was injected to the conduction band of CDs during the cathodic process. The second signal was caused by ion-ion annihilation reactions between TBAB and CDs that produced exciplexes (CDsR). This sensor was highly sensitive to the changes in the surrounding environment and has been demonstrated for iron ion sensing, with limit of detection down to 0.7µM.

Inner filter effect refers to the absorption of the excitation and/or emission light of fluorophores by absorbers in the detection system (Zheng et al., 2013). This mechanism is sensitive, since the change in the absorbance can be transformed exponentially into the change in fluorescence intensity. It requires a good spectral overlap between the absorption band of the absorber and excitation or emission spectrum of the fluorophore. CDs have been tested for their capability as IFE-based fluorescence chemosensor to detect Cr(VI) (Zheng et al., 2013). Cr(VI) absorbed at 260, 360 and 440nm which coincided with the excitation spectrum of CDs at 250 and 358nm and the emission at 456nm. Hence, Cr(VI) can shield the excitation light for CDs and also absorb the light emitted from CDs. Such system has been utilized for the detection of AA due to the AA interaction with Cr(VI) that can hinder the IFE effect.

The CDs-based IFE sensing has been extended for the detection of tumor invasive biomarker β-glucuronide (GLU) (Lu et al., 2016). 4-nitrophenyl-β-D-glucuronide (PNPG) absorbing at 310nm has worked as substrate of GLU while the hydrolysate of PNPG (PNP) has acted as the IFE absorber based on spectral overlap at 410nm. The fluorescence quenching of CDs was used as a measure of the GLU activity and has successfully been applied for clinical human serum samples with high recovery rates between 95.42 to 103.67%.

Biosensors are developed to work on-site and thus subjected to the exposure under different experimental or environmental conditions. Optimization is crucial to ensure the sensing system will work at its optimal conditions. This section covers some of the parameters that are often being optimized for the best performance of the biosensors.

The fluorescence of CDs is usually affected by the pH of the surrounding solution. In general, the fluorescence of CDs was reported to be consistent within the pH range of 5 to 10 (Hou et al., 2013; Wang et al., 2014d; Hou et al., 2015). A detailed study on the effect of pH towards a sensing system for AA utilizing CDs-Fe complex has been studied by Fong and coworkers (Fong et al., 2016). The results showed that the fluorescence quenching of CDs by Fe(III) was effective for pH ranged between 4.5 to 10, but significantly affected under acidic conditions below pH 4. It has been suggested that the surface charge of CDs has played the role due to the carboxyl groups that will be protonated at low pH. A similar observation was reported when the sensor was further utilized for AA sensing.

In biological systems, pH plays an important role as it may affect the structural conformation for biomolecules like proteins, antibodies and enzymes. pH condition is also specific at different biological parts. For instance, the cellular microenvironment for cancer cells is known to be slightly acidic. This can be exploited for the construction of cancer biosensors that were based on detecting change of pH. Shi and coworkers have designed a CDs-based sensor to measure the intracellular pH of whole cells (Shi et al., 2012).

Some fluorophores show a temperature-dependent fluorescence profile due to the changes in surface condition at various temperatures. This applies to CDs as well. Yu and coworkers have demonstrated that fluorescence intensities of CDs were evidently decreasing at elevated temperatures as shown in Fig. 7.13
(Yu et al., 2012). There were dual fluorescence bands and the origins have been attributed to be from the core and surface states.

Detection systems based on fluorescence quenching are often performed at fixed temperature. This is especially for the cases of dynamic quenching that are based on diffusive encounters between the fluorophore and quencher. Higher rates of diffusion occur at elevated temperatures and this leads to higher rate of collisions and subsequently more effective quenching. As a result, the Stern-Volmer constant, KSV of dynamic quenching will be higher when temperature of the system is increased. Unless static quenching is assumed, temperature effect on the sensing performance should be investigated. Most of the CD-based fluorescence detections were based on the assumption of static quenching, due to complex formation as deduced from the Ksv (Xu et al., 2015a) and absorption changes (Mohd Yazid et al., 2013).

The concentration of fluorophore used in a sensing probe has a direct effect on its sensing performance. The general trend of the fluorescence intensities with varying concentration of fluorophore is shown in Fig. 7.14 . A small amount of fluorophore usually results in lower intensities leading to reduced sensitivity. Addition of more fluorophore will yield better intensity but it ends up at an optimum range, giving the highest intensity. Further increase in concentration of the fluorophore from this range often causes saturation leading to self-quenching. CDs show similar trend, and optimization is required to achieve the maximum analytical window for the subsequent detection purposes. For instance, in the work by Wee and coworkers, the highest fluorescence intensity for CDs was determined to be 1:14v/v of CDs to de-ionized water before self-quenching was observed (Wee et al., 2013). Although a similar trend applies for all fluorescent nanoparticles, the exact concentration range can vary significantly. Accordingly, it is essential to determine the effective concentration of CDs to be used for optimal sensing performance.

Biosensors are often designed with sensing receptors that can target a specific analyte, but the efficiency is still relying on the actual sensing environment. Other foreign species present together can possibly interfere with the sensing process via competition interaction leading to false results or ineffective sensing. Therefore, interference study should be carried out with those species that commonly coexist with the analyte. It is ideal to mimic the real matrix condition for the targeted environment, as well as the rational concentration range of foreign species that needs to be tested. In some cases, it is more advisable to test the interference effect in the presence of the analyte rather than testing it solely on the potential interfering species. The interfering species can possibly block the sensing receptor but not necessarily cause any change in the readable signal. If this happens, the intended analyte will be shielded from interacting with the receptors, giving a false impression that the target analyte is absent. The tolerance limit can be evaluated by observing the maximum concentration of interfering species that can be added before the initial signal generated from the analyte deviates for more than 5% (Zhu et al., 2016c).

The performance of a biosensor is also judged by its repeatability for reuse and the consistency in giving repeated readings. The significance of the data must be validated with statistical study and error analysis. The precision and repeatability of the sensing data can be represented by evaluating the standard deviation of the sample readings. Typically, triplicate readings taken with percentage standard deviation within ±5% are considered a good sensing probe.

The recovery rate is important, as it signifies the workability of the sensing probe when applied in real samples that are subject to interfering effect from the matrix. Usually the response of a biosensor will be validated against reading from a conventional instrument in order to evaluate the recovery value. In cases where real sample is found to show low or no content of the analyte, spiking can be done to evaluate the recovery of the biosensor. This is performed by adding a standard: analyte of a known concentration into the real samples, such as tap water, human serum, or urine samples. The recovery rate is represented as the percentage of the measured concentration deviated from the known added amount spiked. For example, Zhu and coworkers have evaluated the recovery of 103.6% from their histidine sensor using N-doped CDs (Zhu et al., 2016c). This was done by adding a known amount of 10.00μM of histidine into human serum samples and performed the detection using the sensor that was found to give the concentration reading of 10.14μM. The relative standard deviation of the sensor was calculated at 2.3%.

Sensors based on CDs have been developed to target a variety of analytes. The most commonly detected is metal ions such as Hg(II) (Gonçalves et al., 2010; Wang et al., 2015f; Yu et al., 2015) and Fe(III) (Han et al., 2016a; Wang et al., 2016b, 2017; Qu et al., 2013). Small molecules that participate in the cellular activities have also been studied. This includes thrombin as a diagnostic biomarker for cardiovascular diseases (Wang et al., 2011d; Zhang et al., 2013a); dopamine as the neurotransmitter in the central nervous system (Mao et al., 2012; Huang et al., 2013a; Zhu et al., 2016a; Tian et al., 2017); glutathione as requisite endogenous antioxidant (Shi et al., 2014b; Cai et al., 2015; Yang et al., 2015a; Gupta et al., 2016; Huang et al., 2016); acetylcholine as the chemical released by nerve cells (Wang et al., 2016a), and adenosine 5′-triphosphate (ATP) as the mediator of energy transfer in metabolic processes (Xu et al., 2016b).

There have been a number of studies that revolved around cancer research. Cancer biomarkers or tumor-associated antigens are critical for early diagnosis and prognosis of cancers. Aptamer and antibodies that can specifically bind to targeted biological analyte have been conjugated to CDs and employed for the sensing of cancer biomarkers, such as carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA) in human blood samples (Miao et al., 2016a), protein biomarker mucin 1 (MUC1) (Ma et al., 2015), and human IgG antigen (Wu et al., 2015). Besides direct quantification of biomarkers, there are also reports on fluorometric assays using CDs to assess cancer-related enzymatic activities. For example, the activity of alkaline phosphatase (ALP) that is involved in signal transduction has been used to detect breast and prostatic cancers (Lorente et al., 1999). On a different study, an assay based on β-cyclodextrin modified CDs has been developed for real-time detection of ALP activity (Tang et al., 2016).

Besides detecting cancers, CD sensing probes have also been applied for evaluation of other catalytic activities such as glutathione reductase (Zhu et al., 2016b) and acetylcholinesterase activity related to neurodegenerative diseases (Rong et al., 2015). Other than adopting CD sensing in disease diagnosis, it has been extended for the detection of pharmaceutical products. The extensive current use of antibiotics may have raised serious attention of the public due to the allergy and resistance issues. Therefore, rapid fluorometric detection based on CDs has been used to test antibiotics such as D-penicillamine (Yuan et al., 2016a), ampicillin (Mishra et al., 2016), and kanamycin (Wang et al., 2016c).

Bioimaging is a powerful tool for visualizing microscopic details of living species especially cells and tissues. CDs can serve as a promising fluorescent tag for several commonly employed imaging techniques, which include fluorescence microscopy, confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM), multiphoton microscopy, and magnetic resonance microscopy (MRM).

Fluorescence microscopy enables superior sensitivity for imaging of cells and molecular biology at subcellular levels. A specific excitation band that is usually of shorter wavelength will be focused to the sample while the emission at longer wavelength will be collected by the microscope objective to picture the fluorescence image (Lichtman and Conchello, 2005). Better contrast can be achieved by using specific fluorophores that accumulate at the area of interest (Smith et al., 2010). In view of this, CDs are considered as a promising alternative due to their high photostability and low toxicity when compared to the conventional fluorescent proteins, organic dyes and QDs. For instance, Wang and coworkers have prepared organosilane-functionalized CDs that have been further fabricated into silica-encapsulated nanoparticles to label human gastric carcinoma BGC823 cells with low cytotoxicity (Wang et al., 2011a).

Jana and coworkers have reported the use of highly fluorescent CDs prepared by simple one-step hydrothermal treatment in “live-dead” assay for fibroblast like cells, L929 (Jana et al., 2016). Their work showed that fluorescence of CDs could clearly be seen only in viable cells but not in dead cells. Chen and coworkers have also fabricated N- and S-codoped CDs that could be utilized for multicolor fluorescence imaging of human cervical carcinoma HeLa cells with low cytotoxicity and good biocompatibility (Chen et al., 2017).

An earlier version of confocal microscope is equipped with a pinhole or spatial filter placed in front of a zirconium arc light source and an objective lens (Minsky, 1957, 1988). The zirconium arc light source was later replaced with laser to produce images via point-by-point scanning. The signal from the specimen is detected by a photomultiplier tube (PMT). A pinhole is usually included to eliminate the unwanted background fluorescence (Paddock, 1999). Later, this was known as the confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM) and commonly employed for imaging of both fixed and viable cells or tissues as well as to monitor the dynamic processes of living cells, such as synaptic activity and cell death (Paddock, 2000; Asbury, 2011). When compared to conventional fluorescence microscopy, CLSM enables deep tissue penetration at a finite length of 200–300µm and provides images with better resolution at depth (Smith et al., 2010).

Xue and coworkers revealed that CDs exhibited bright blue, green, and red emission as shown in Fig. 7.15 , which could be utilized for multicolor live liver hepatocellular carcinoma HepG2 cells imaging (Xue et al., 2016).
