Furthermore, CDs have been successfully used for the imaging of HeLa cells, human embryonic kidney HEK 293 cells, macrophages RAW264.7 cells, human umbilical vein endothelial HUVEC cells, human breast cancer Bcap-37, and MCF-7 cells (Bandi et al., 2016; Gong et al., 2016; Hu et al., 2016; Shen et al., 2016; Ramanan et al., 2016; Yan et al., 2016).

Multiphoton microscopy enables imaging of living and turbid specimens within a few hundred micrometers depth. Multiphoton microscopy relies on nonlinear interactions, which involve simultaneous absorption of two or more photons generated through NIR femtosecond pulsed lasers excitation by a single fluorophore (Hoover and Squier, 2013).

Two-photon excitation fluorescence microscopy (TPM) was invented by Denk and coworkers after predicting the possibility of two-photon absorption by Maria Göppert-Mayer in 1931 (Denk et al., 1990). CDs passivated with PPEI-EI exhibiting two-photon excitations could be applied for multiphoton bioimaging of MCF-7 cells (Cao et al., 2007). Furthermore, N-doped CDs were also employed as two-photon fluorescent probes for multiphoton imaging of HeLa cells in deep tissue (Liu et al., 2013). In vivo tracking of human adipose-derived stem cells using athymic mice models employing multiphoton imaging has also been reported (Kim et al., 2016).

In addition to TPM, other powerful techniques in multiphoton microscopy include three-photon excitation and second harmonic generation (SHG). Three-photon excitation involves simultaneous absorption of three photons whose combined energy is sufficient to induce a molecular transition to an excited state (Ustione and Piston, 2011). Whereas SHG is a second order coherent process involving up-conversion of two lower energy photons to twice the incident frequency of an excitation laser (Campagnola, 2011). However, until now, there is no known report on the application of CDs for both three-photon excitation and SHG.

Magnetic resonance microscopy, or sometimes known as magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) is a noninvasive technology widely used in clinical practice (Dashevsky et al., 2015). MRI is neither dependent on sample thickness nor opacity; instead it relies on the magnetic field strength (Burg et al., 2002). An hydrogen atom is commonly used for imaging in MRI since it is a solitary proton that can generate a small magnetic field and interact with an external magnetic field (Hendee and Morgan, 1984).

Gandium-doped CDs exhibiting enhanced blue fluorescence has been successfully demonstrated for MRI and fluorescence bimodal imaging of HeLa cells (Yuan et al., 2016b). In addition, MRI and fluorescence bimodal imaging of baby hamster kidney BHK cells and ovarian cancer HO-8910 cells have also been reported utilizing water soluble magnetite (Fe3O4)-CDs and manganese (II)-CDs hybrid respectively, (Li et al., 2016a, Han et al., 2016b). Considerable studies have shown evidence that CDs have great potential as MRI/fluorescence contrast agent for bimodal imaging.

In a pioneer work by Sun and coworkers, bioimaging of human colon colorectal Caco2 cells using CDs via confocal microscopy has been reported (Sun et al., 2006). The PPEI-EI-functionalized CDs were found to be readily taken up by the cells and exhibited bright green fluorescence. Many studies have directly applied CDs for bioimaging of diverse cell lines such as human breast cancer Bcap-37 cells, human breast adenocarcinoma MCF-7 cells, human colon adenocarcinoma SW480 cells and human hepatocellular carcinoma SMMC-7721 cells without further functionalization (Jiang et al., 2014; Hu et al., 2016; Ramanan et al., 2016; Song et al., 2016).

CDs exhibiting multicolor fluorescence when excited at different wavelengths are also reported for tumor cell imaging. Bandi and coworkers have demonstrated that the brightly fluorescent CDs exhibited blue, green, and red emission obtained from onion waste via simple autoclave method can serve as fluorescent probes for HeLa cells imaging using LSCM (Bandi et al., 2016). The CDs were further employed as sensing probe for intracellular detection of Fe(III) ions where the fluorescence of CDs was quenched in the presence of Fe(III) ions.

Surface passivation is necessary to tailor the properties of CDs for specific targeted bioimaging applications. Acid-catalyzed CDs conjugated with DNA aptamer, Mucin-1 (MUC-1) via EDC/NHS reaction have been reported for targeted imaging of MCF-7 cells (Wang et al., 2016d). The MUC-1 conjugated CDs were found to specifically bind MCF-7 cells. Furthermore, PEI-passivated CDs with noncovalently conjugated FA can act as specific fluorescent “turn-on” probe for FR-positive cancer cells (Lei et al., 2016). Their confocal fluorescence microscopy images illustrated that FR-positive HeLa and HepG2 cells exhibited bright blue fluorescence, whereas no obvious fluorescence was observed in FR-negative MCF-7 cells. Bhunia and coworkers also prepared CDs via simple one-step hydrothermal treatment of FA solution for targeted imaging of folate receptors-expressing cells (Bhunia et al., 2016).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Bacterial, fungal and yeast cell imaging
ce:section.ce:para: Rapid and sensitive detections of bacteria are essential in areas such as microbiology, food safety, and medical diagnosis. CDs have also been explored for the imaging of bacteria. Selective microscopic visualization of Escherichia coli based on the specific interaction between mannose units on CDs and FimH proteins on the tip of fimbriae of E. coli were reported in several studies (Weng et al., 2015; Wang et al., 2016b; Lai et al., 2016). In a separate study, CDs have been employed successfully as fluorescence markers and enabled rapid determination of bacteria content in sewage water (Mandal and Parvin, 2011). The development has reached the advancement where differentiation among various bacteria strains was possible, based on the distinct fluorescence emission profiles attributed to the different molecular properties of the bacterial membranes (Nandi et al., 2015).

As for fungal cell imaging, CDs were found to readily internalized into plant pathogenic fungal Valsa mali Miyabe et Yamada cells (Jin et al., 2015). Due to the pH-sensitive and excellent photostability properties, the CDs could be a potential probe for long duration dynamic imaging in viable fungal cells. In addition, Kailasa and coworkers have successfully used the green and red-emitting CDs for fungal cells, Aspergillus aculeatus and Fusarium avenaceum imaging (Kasibabu et al., 2015a, Kasibabu et al., 2015b). CDs were also found to be effectively internalized by fungal cells and homogenously distributed in the cell membrane, cytoplasm, and nucleus (Mehta et al., 2015; Bhamore et al., 2016).

Other than bacterial and fungal cells, CDs have been adopted for yeast cell labeling. CDs synthesized from gum Arabic have been utilized for yeast, Saccharomyces cerevisiae, imaging (Thakur et al., 2014). In this work, the yeast cells showed bright green fluorescence when excited at 350nm, corresponding to the fluorescence of CDs. Pandey and coworkers have also successfully labeled yeast cells using mesoporous silica oxide/CDs complex that exhibited green fluorescence (Pandey et al., 2014). Furthermore, confocal laser microscopic images by Mehta and coworkers have shown that CDs could be effectively taken up by yeast cells, possibly through endocytosis mechanism and exhibited strong blue, green and red fluorescence upon excitation at 405, 488, and 561nm (Mehta et al., 2014). The results showed that the CDs were well dispersed and located in the yeast nucleus.

Preliminary in vitro studies have convincingly shown that CDs can act as promising fluorescence probes for bioimaging and biolabeling applications. This has encouraged further exploration on using CDs as nanoprobes for in vivo optical imaging. Yang and coworkers have pioneered the in vivo bioimaging for mice as animal models using CDs (Yang et al., 2009a). CDs can generally be introduced into mice models via three different routes namely, subcutaneous, intravenous, and intramuscular injections. The bio-distribution and clearance in mice models upon injection of CDs have been widely investigated for better understanding of their in vivo kinetic behaviors that is crucial for clinical translation. CDs were found to accumulate mainly in kidney and liver (Yang et al., 2009a; Huang et al., 2013b; Chong et al., 2014; Parvin and Mandal, 2016). Some studies have also reported that CDs could be accumulated in other organs such as spleen, lung, and heart (Yang et al., 2010a; Tao et al., 2012; Nurunnabi et al., 2013a,b). In addition, CDs usually are found to be excreted rapidly from the body of the tested animals. Huang and coworkers claimed that the CDs were completely cleared out from mice after 24hours regardless of the injection route (Huang et al., 2013b).

Toxicity of CDs as in vivo imaging probes is also of great concern. Zheng and coworkers have conducted systematic single- and repeated-dose toxicity evaluations utilizing CA-based CDs in mice (Zheng et al., 2015b). The results revealed that both single and repeated dose have exhibited relatively low in vivo toxicity. Similarly, a study by Chen and coworkers found that the health conditions of the tested mice showed no significant alterations after being continuously administrated with bare CDs and PEGylated CDs (Chen et al., 2015). Another fascinating finding is on the use of CDs for in vivo tumor cell imaging in several xenograft tumor mouse models (Yang et al., 2010b, Wang et al., 2015b, Xiong et al., 2015). Fluorescence images of glioma-bearing mice in the study by Ruan and coworkers have illustrated that CDs would accumulate in the glioma site of the brain but not in normal brain tissue (Ruan et al., 2014). Utilizing CDs as in vitro and in vivo fluorescence-based nano-thermometer was also demonstrated based on the temperature-dependent fluorescence emission of CDs (Yang et al., 2015c).

Zebrafish is also a widely used vertebrate model for in vivo optical imaging due to their well-defined developmental stages and high homology with mammals (Ko et al., 2011). CDs could be introduced into zebrafish through swallowing, skin-absorption, and microinjection. Embryonic development of zebrafish can be visualized by employing CDs as imaging probes (Xu et al., 2014b, Kang et al., 2015). CDs often claimed to exhibit tissue-dependent affinity in zebrafish and found preferentially accumulated in the head, yolk sac, and tail (Xu et al., 2014b, Shi et al., 2014a, Kang et al., 2015). Furthermore, CDs have found applications to selectively label apoptotic cells in live zebrafish with bright red PL, as well as to detect oxidative damage induced by DOX in the digestive system (Wu et al., 2014; Roy et al., 2015). In a recent study, Li and coworkers have reported that CDs could bind specifically to calcified bone structures of live larvae of zebrafish, leading to strong fluorescence enhancement (Li et al., 2016d). Specific in vivo imaging for bone is important to the study the molecular mechanisms of bone disease (Haugeberg, 2008). Besides zebrafish, fluorescent imaging of guppy fish by feeding the fish with CDs mixed fish food was also possible, as shown in Fig. 7.16 (Jiang et al., 2014).

CDs introduced via oral ingestion have showed no remarkable toxic effect throughout the life cycle of insects. A study by Parvin and coworkers has shown that CDs exhibiting multicolor fluorescence were used for in vivo imaging for the internal organs of Drosophila Melanogaster at different life cycle stages (Parvin et al., 2013). Moreover, CDs can also be used for the imaging of nematode Caenorhabditis elegans via oral admission (Pramanik et al., 2016). The performance of CDs as nontoxic contrast agents is competitive to those well-developed QDs for in vivo applications (Yang et al., 2009b, Cao et al., 2012).

CDs are shown to be effective nano-vectors for delivery of drugs and genes in therapeutic treatments. CDs have also been exploited as effective carriers as well as photosensitizing agents for photo-thermal and photodynamic therapies.

CDs can act as nanocarriers or tracers for drug delivery systems. Studies have shown that DOX could be conjugated to CDs and showed a controlled release profile upon in vitro internalization by cancer cells such as human lung adenocarcinoma A549 cells, human oral squamous carcinoma H357 cells and HeLa cells (Yang et al., 2016b; Samantara et al., 2016; Sun et al., 2017). Doxorubicin is a widely used anthracycline chemotherapeutic agent for cancer treatment by intercalating DNA and inhibits the biosynthesis of macromolecules in tumor cells (Kang et al., 2016). The controlled release behavior was achieved based on the pH-dependent property where the release rate was designed to be higher at around pH 5. Accordingly, release will be triggered at the infected area since cancer cells tend to be more acidic compared to normal cells (Sun et al., 2017).

In order to enhance the specificity for targeted drug delivery, surface modification of CDs with navigational molecules is required. CDs can be conjugated with FA before loaded with chemotherapeutic agent, where such a system was found specifically internalized by HeLa cells and simultaneously reduced cytotoxicity to nontarget cells (Mewada et al., 2014; Wang et al., 2014e). Similarly, the conjugation of CDs with HA and Tf that bind specifically to hyaluronan CD44 receptors and Tf receptors respectively has found effective as cancer targeting agents in CDs-based delivery systems (Lee et al., 2013; Li et al., 2016c).

Simultaneous dual-modal of bioimaging and targeted drug delivery utilizing CDs as nanoplatforms have also been reported. Deng and coworkers have fabricated a CD-based fluorescence trackable nanovector for controlled release of nitric oxide (NO) and targeted intracellular delivery, since NO is crucial for regulation of some physiological and pathophysiological processes (Deng et al., 2016). Dual modality intracellular imaging and targeted delivery of chemotherapy and anticancer drugs, such as cisplatin, epirubicin, gemcitabine, berberine hydrochloride (BHC), oxaliplatin, flutamide and boldine to cancer cells, were also reported (Nigam et al., 2014; Zheng et al., 2014; Matai et al., 2015; Feng et al., 2016; Thakur et al., 2016; D’souza et al., 2016a,b).

Photo-responsive nano-drug delivery system designed with photo-trigger molecules such as quinoline has also been reported for cancer treatment (Karthik et al., 2013). CDs and the anticancer drug were loaded into the delivery system and have achieved effective drug release by the quinoline chlorambucil (Qucbl) as photo-trigger upon irradiation. The controlled release triggered by redox potential is also possible. It was reported by Jiao and coworkers that the release of DOX from CD-based nanocarrier has been enhanced by the presence of GSH (Jiao et al., 2016). Glutathione is notably high in concentration in many tumor cells. Drug release triggered by ATP has also been reported, where anticancer drugs loaded on aptamer conjugated nanocomposite was released upon internalization of the nanocomposite into ATP-rich cytoplasm of targeted tumor cells (Zheng et al., 2015a). The high stimuli-responsive drug release behavior is particularly useful for simultaneous drug therapy and tracking of the specific site utilizing CDs.

Gene therapy holds promising potential towards effective treatment by altering the expression of dysregulated genes to control diseases such as genetic disorders, cancers, and acquired immunodeficiency syndrome (AIDS) (Keles et al., 2016). A typical gene therapy formulation composes of a gene vector and a genetic material. Naked nucleic acid is an example of genetic material and should be protected by an effective vector to avoid enzymatic degradation during the delivery. The vector should be degraded to release the naked nucleic acid in its functional form for transfection upon arrival in nucleus (Loh et al., 2016). The gene delivery carrier can generally be classified into viral and nonviral vectors. Although viral vectors offer high transfection efficacy, nonviral vectors are more desired due to critical safety concerns (Guo and Huang, 2011).

CDs have good potential to be utilized as gene delivery vectors. Liu and coworkers have prepared PEI functionalized CDs through one-step microwave-assisted pyrolysis and demonstrated successful an in vitro plasmid DNA transfection in African green monkey kidney COS-7 cells and human hepatocellular carcinoma HepG2 cells (Liu et al., 2012a). Polyethylenimine is one of the most widely applied cationic compounds for gene delivery because it facilitates complex formation with gene, enhances cellular uptake and aids in endosomal escape via proton sponge effect (Kichler et al., 2001; Yue et al., 2011). Furthermore, there is also report on the transfection of plasmid DNA containing green fluorescent protein (GFP) as reporter gene targeting breast cancer cells using PEI-modified CDs as gene carrier (Misra et al., 2015). The result showed that utilization of PEI-functionalized CDs as gene vector markedly reduced the cytotoxicity when compared to bare PEI.

In vivo studies are also crucial to pave a way towards actualizing the utilization of CDs as efficient vector in gene therapy, although most of the current studies are still at the in vitro stage. The feasibility of CDs-based gene delivery system in vivo has been demonstrated by employing mice models (Wang et al., 2014c). Post functionalization of CDs with low molecular weight PEI modified with hydrophobic parts, alkyl-PEI2k for delivery of siRNA and DNA in xenografted tumor mice showed low toxicity with high delivery efficiency. Pierrat and coworkers have also explored the utilization of bPEI25k-functionalized CDs for siRNA delivery and reported high transgene expression in the lung of mice (Pierrat et al., 2015).

Photo-thermal therapy is a minimally invasive and potentially effective therapeutic treatment that has nowadays attracted massive attention. Photo-thermal therapy relies on activation of photosensitizing agents by pulsed laser irradiation at NIR to generate heat for thermal ablation of cancer tumors (Chen et al., 2016b). When compared to the conventional radiotherapy or chemotherapy, the key merits of PTT include the capability for deep tissue penetration and minimal effect of nonselective cell death on the surrounding healthy tissue (Jaque et al., 2014). An ideal photosensitizing agent should possess large absorption cross sections for optical wavelengths, low toxicity, ease in functionalization, and highly soluble in biocompatible solutions (Jaque et al., 2014).

CDs seem to fulfill most of the requirements for PTT. A pioneer work by Zhang and coworkers has reported the in vitro PTT application of CDs for cancer cells treatment (Tu et al., 2014). It was observed that PEGylated-CDs exhibited strong photo-thermal conversion capability with insignificant cytotoxicity to MCF-7 cells upon NIR irradiation. Later works have demonstrated that CDs could be integrated into silicon dioxide (SiO2) nanocomposites loaded with DOX for combined PTT and chemotherapy in cancer cells (Tu et al., 2016). In vivo experiment in this study showed that tumor growth in mice models could be effectively inhibited without cancer recurrence.

Several studies have also reported the combination of PTT and chemotherapy based on CDs nanoplatforms for the delivery of DOX in cancer cells treatment (Pandey et al., 2013; Wang et al., 2015c; Singh et al., 2016). In addition to DOX, a lysosome-targeting NO donor and FA-directing group were incorporated onto CDs for targeted delivery of NO to FR-positive cancer cell lines, and simultaneously induced rapid increase in temperature upon laser irradiation for PTT (Xiang et al., 2016). Combining PTT and gene therapy for cancer treatment was also explored. Therapeutic gene, pING4 was conjugated to CDs via electrostatic interaction for targeted gene delivery to tumor cells both in vitro and in vivo and at the same time, the CDs were acting as photo-thermal converter to induce tumor cells death (Meng et al., 2016). Moreover, simultaneous photoacoustic imaging based on the detection of ultrasound waves and photo-thermal ablation of cancer cells utilizing CDs-based nanoplatforms was also possible (Miao et al., 2016b). Considerable studies have shown that CDs possess great potential as photosensitizing agents for PTT as well as enhancing the synergistic effect of combined therapy.

Photodynamic therapy is another therapeutic treatment that involves the activation of photosensitizers by specific light source to induce death on targeted cells. The aforementioned PTT involves generation of heat whereas PDT involves generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS). The three main components in PDT include a specific light source that provides energy, photosensitizers that conduct PDT reaction, and oxygen-containing molecules that produce ROS (Zhou et al., 2016). Photodynamic therapy relies on irradiation of light at specific wavelength upon localization of photosensitizing agents within targeted tumors. Subsequently, ROS such as singlet oxygen (SO) or free radicals will be generated to induce cell death and tissue destruction (Hong et al., 2015). Similar to PTT, specific localization of photosensitizers in targeted tumors can minimize collateral damage to the surrounding normal tissue (Fowley et al., 2013).

CDs also have been used as photosensitizing agents for PDT. A commonly used PDT photosensitizer, chlorin e6 (Ce6) has been incorporated into CDs system via redox-triggered cleavable disulfide linkage (Du et al., 2016; Li et al., 2016f). The CDs-based system that was initially nonfluorescent and remained photo inactive would undergo reduction cleavage of the disulfide linker when exposed to tumor relevant GSH. Consequently, Ce6 was detached from the CDs-based system and would recover photo activity to mediate PDT in HeLa cells. Both in vitro and in vivo results showed remarkably effective suppression of tumor growth. In addition, CDs exhibiting efficient FRET for two-photon excited PDT with enhanced therapeutic effect have also been revealed in in vitro experiments employing HeLa and HepG2 cell lines, as well as in vivo animal study using radiation-induced fibrosarcoma (RIF-1) mouse tumor model (Fowley et al., 2013; Wang et al., 2014b; Yang et al., 2016a).

Simultaneous imaging and PDT using CD-based systems have also been achieved. Ce6-conjugated CDs could be employed for real time imaging of photosensitizing agent in vivo for enhanced photosensitizer fluorescence detection (PFD) and at the same time, mediating PDT by FRET mechanism (Huang et al., 2012b). The efficacy of PDT in cancer treatment can be further improved with the convergence of PDT and PTT. Nurunnabi and coworkers have prepared carboxylated CDs that could act as photo-sensitizers to destroy both in vitro and in vivo cancer cells by thermal ablation as well as generate singlet oxygen for PDT effect (Nurunnabi et al., 2014). The obtained carboxylated CDs also enabled concurrent noninvasive optical imaging of deep tissue and tumors. The same concept has been demonstrated by integrating CDs in the SiO2-based nanocomposites to act as both fluorescence imaging and PDT agents, as shown in Fig. 7.17 . The results revealed that the CD-integrated nanocomposites could serve as a promising phototheranostic agent with enhanced cancer cell death by employing synergistic PDT and PTT.

In summary, many studies have reported various synthetic approaches to produce CDs, which have differed by the starting materials, synthesis mechanisms, and experimental conditions. All these have generated better insights into ways to obtain better quality CDs with more controlled physical, chemical, and optical characteristics. CDs have excellent luminescent properties, such as strong fluorescence in the visible region upon excitation at UV light, and excellent photostability. Such optical property coupled with their biocompatibility, low cytotoxicity, and ease of functionalization have deemed CDs very good candidates as light emitters for biomedical applications. They have successfully been demonstrated for various biosensing, bioimaging and therapeutic applications.

There are still a lot of investigations required to further develop CDs into useful and practical biomedical applications. Most of the biosensors developed using CDs were proven in the solution form which practical reusability of the sensing probe is limited. For future works, the potential of CDs as light emitters for optical biosensors can be improvized and further fabricated for portable sensors, eventually applicable as point-of-care diagnostic kits. For the use of CDs as nanocarrier and imaging agent for theranostic applications, more solid investigations must be carried out to establish better performance of CDs in this area. For in vivo applications, the efficiency of drug delivery to the targeted site should be improved to ensure that negative impacts on healthy cells can be minimized.
